questions.question: Describe the authentication flow in OAuth 2.0 and explain access token vs refresh token.
questions.answer: OAuth 2.0 allows apps to access user resources without seeing passwords. The flow: user visits your app, clicks 'login with Google', you're redirected to Google, user grants permission, Google redirects back with authorization code, you exchange code for access token (short-lived, grants resource access) and refresh token (long-lived, obtains new access tokens). Access tokens API requests directly; when expired, use refresh token to get new access token without re-authenticating the user.
questions.explanation: OAuth 2.0 solves the 'password sharing' problem—you don't need user's password to access their data. The authorization code flow is most secure: the authorization server returns a one-time code (not the token directly), which your backend exchanges for tokens. This prevents token exposure in browser history or logs. Access tokens are short-lived (typically 1 hour) for security—if compromised, limited window of exposure. Refresh tokens live longer (days/weeks) but are stored securely server-side, never exposed to the browser in frontend-only flows. JWT is common for access tokens—self-contained, can be validated without calling authorization server. Refresh token rotation issues new refresh tokens with each use, invalidating old ones to limit stolen token usefulness. PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange) adds security for public clients like mobile apps.

questions.question: Explain the differences between symmetric and asymmetric encryption, and when each is used.
questions.answer: Symmetric encryption uses a single secret key for both encryption and decryption, making it fast and suitable for bulk data. Asymmetric encryption uses a key pair: a public key for encryption and a private key for decryption. Symmetric is used for encrypting large amounts of data (like TLS session keys), while asymmetric handles key exchange and digital signatures. In HTTPS, asymmetric encryption initially exchanges a symmetric session key, then symmetric encrypts the actual data transfer.
questions.explanation: Symmetric algorithms like AES-256 are computationally efficient and can encrypt gigabytes per second, making them ideal for encrypting stored data or ongoing communications. The challenge is key distribution—sharing the secret securely. Asymmetric algorithms like RSA or ECC solve this through key pairs. The public key can be shared openly; only the holder of the private key can decrypt. This enables secure key exchange without prior shared secrets. Hybrid systems combine both: RSA or ECDH exchanges a symmetric key, then AES encrypts the actual data. This balances security with performance. Modern TLS uses this pattern—ECDHE for key exchange provides forward secrecy, while AES-GCM encrypts the tunnel. Understanding when to use each type is fundamental to cryptographic system design.

questions.question: How does CSRF protection work with SameSite cookies, and what are the limitations?
questions.answer: SameSite cookies restrict cross-origin requests by telling browsers only to send cookies on same-site requests. SameSite=Strict blocks all cross-site cookie sending—most secure but breaks legitimate cross-site flows. SameSite=Lax allows cookies on top-level navigations (GET requests) only. SameSite=None allows cross-site cookies but requires Secure (HTTPS-only). CSRF tokens provide additional protection by requiring a unique token in requests that attackers cannot replicate since they can't read the cookie value.
questions.explanation: Before SameSite, CSRF relied on cookies being sent automatically with every request—browsers couldn't distinguish legitimate requests from forged ones. Attackers tricked users into submitting forms to target sites, carrying the user's cookies automatically. SameSite cookies break this by letting sites opt-out of cross-site sending. However, limitations exist: older browsers don't support SameSite, some browser modes exempt localhost, and some use cases genuinely need cross-site cookies (third-party integrations). CSRF tokens solve these gaps—each form includes a server-generated token that must match the session token. The attacker cannot read the token due to Same-Origin Policy, so their forged requests lack the required value. Defense-in-depth uses both: SameSite as default protection, CSRF tokens for sensitive actions and browser compatibility. The Synchronizer Token Pattern generates unique per-request tokens; Double Submit Cookie sends token in both cookie and header for stateless implementations.

questions.question: What is Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) and how does output encoding prevent it?
questions.answer: XSS injects malicious scripts into web pages viewed by other users through unescaped user input in HTML output. Output encoding converts special characters into safe HTML entities—for example, < becomes < and > becomes >. When browsers render encoded output as text rather than executable HTML/JavaScript, injected scripts cannot run. This is the primary defense for reflected and stored XSS, neutralizing scripts before they reach the user's browser.
questions.explanation: XSS occurs when applications include untrusted data in HTML without proper encoding. Attackers craft inputs containing script tags or event handlers that execute when other users view the content. Three types exist: stored XSS (saved to database, affects all visitors), reflected XSS (in URL parameters, immediate victim), and DOM-based XSS (client-side manipulation). Output encoding context matters—HTML encoding for body content, attribute encoding for tag attributes, JavaScript encoding for inline scripts, and URL encoding for query parameters. Modern frameworks like React and Angular auto-encode by default, but developers can bypass this with dangerouslySetInnerHTML or v-html directives. Content Security Policy headers provide additional defense by restricting script sources. Comprehensive XSS prevention requires context-aware output encoding plus CSP as defense-in-depth.

questions.question: What is OWASP Top 10? Name 3 critical vulnerabilities.
questions.answer: OWASP Top 10 lists most critical web security risks. Key ones: 1) Injection (SQL, NoSQL) - untrusted data sent to interpreter. 2) Broken Authentication - weak credentials, session management. 3) XSS - malicious scripts injected into web pages.
questions.explanation: Prevention: parameterized queries for injection, strong session management, Content Security Policy for XSS. Regular security audits against OWASP guidelines reduce attack surface significantly.
